hook: You already know how to work with individual numbers and equations — but what happens when you need to add up 100 terms, count millions of possible passwords, or predict the likelihood of winning a game? This unit bridges the gap between algebra and real-world reasoning about patterns, large-scale counting, and chance. By the end, you'll have powerful tools that show up everywhere from finance to computer science to statistics.
concept: This unit is about structured counting and summation — recognizing that many real-world situations involve ordered or grouped collections of numbers, and that mathematics gives us elegant shortcuts to analyze them, captured formally in the ideas of discrete mathematics and combinatorics.

topic_roadmap.description: You will identify and generate arithmetic and geometric sequences, find any term using explicit formulas, and recognize the patterns that distinguish constant differences from constant ratios.
Master writing and evaluating sums of sequences using sigma notation $\sum$, including formulas for finite arithmetic and geometric series and, when it converges, an infinite geometric series.
Apply the Binomial Theorem to expand expressions of the form $(a+b)^n$ quickly and efficiently, connecting Pascal's Triangle to the binomial coefficients $\binom{n}{k}$.
You will use the Fundamental Counting Principle, permutations, and combinations to count arrangements and selections, distinguishing when order matters and when it does not.
You will calculate theoretical and experimental probabilities of events, applying counting techniques to find the likelihood of outcomes in real-world scenarios.
Learn to construct formal proofs by mathematical induction, rigorously verifying that statements involving $n$ — such as series formulas — hold true for all positive integers.
